docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This project provides a comprehensive automation framework to test router performance under load using Raspberry Pi as the controller. It simulates multiple virtual network clients, performs parallel pings, executes simultaneous downloads, and monitors Raspberry Pi health metrics — all in real time.

docs.title: What This Project Does
docs.text:
• Creates virtual network clients using Linux network namespaces.
  • Simulates parallel ping tests to measure connectivity.
  • Runs simultaneous download tests to stress-test bandwidth and routing.
  • Monitors Raspberry Pi's CPU, RAM, Disk, Temperature, and Load every 2 seconds.
  • Logs everything with a clean, structured format for analysis.
  • Allows integrating tests through Behave (BDD).

docs.title: Why This Project
docs.text:
Routers often behave differently under high client load. By creating multiple isolated namespaces, you simulate dozens of clients without real hardware. This is useful for:

  • Network load testing
  • Router firmware testing
  • Performance benchmarking
  • Automated QA for network devices

docs.title: Programming & Frameworks
docs.text:
• Python 3 (asyncio-based parallel execution)
  • Behave (Gherkin) for BDD test scenarios
  • Linux Network Namespace for the virtual client creation
  • MkDocs + Material Theme for documentation

docs.title: Linux Networking Tools
docs.text:
• ip netns, macvlan, veth for virtual clients
  • ping, wget for network operations

docs.title: Hardware Requirement
docs.text:
• Raspberry Pi 4/3 running Raspberry Pi OS
  • Stable Internet Connection
  • Router
  • SSH Access Recommended [Not neccessary]

docs.title: Raspberry Pi Setup Requirements
docs.text: Before running tests, ensure your Raspberry Pi meets the following:

docs.title: 1. Install Required Packages
docs.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install python3 python3-pip python3-venv net-tools iputils-ping wget -y

docs.title: 2. Install Python Dependencies
docs.text:
pip3 install psutil behave mkdocs mkdocs-material

After running tests, namespaces will appear here.

docs.title: 3. Project Folder Structure
docs.text:
Stress-test-router/
│
├── src/
│   ├── ping_manager.py
│   ├── download_manager.py
│   ├── health_monitor.py
│   └── ...
│
├── utils/
│   └── logger.py
│
├── features/
│   ├── steps/
│   └── test files...
│
├── docs/
│   └── mkdocs documentation
│
└── config.yaml

docs.title: What Happens When You Run the Project
docs.text: Below is the complete execution flow:

docs.title: 1. Namespace Creation
docs.text:
The system creates N virtual clients using macvlan:

sudo ip netns add client1
sudo ip link add link eth0 client1-macvlan type macvlan mode bridge
sudo ip link set client1-macvlan netns client1

Each namespace becomes an isolated client.

docs.title: 2. Ping Test Execution
docs.text:
Each client pings a target IP (e.g., 8.8.8.8) for X seconds:

client1 → ping 8.8.8.8
client2 → ping 8.8.8.8
...

All run in parallel using asyncio.

docs.title: 3. Download Test Execution
docs.text:
Each namespace downloads the same file simultaneously using wget. This stresses:

  • LAN ↔ Router performance
  • Firewall/NAT table efficiency
  • Bandwidth distribution

docs.title: 4. Real-Time Raspberry Pi Monitoring
docs.text:
Every 2 seconds the Pi reports:

  • CPU Utilization
  • Temperature (if sensor available)
  • RAM Usage
  • Disk Usage
  • System Load

Example log:

[PI HEALTH] CPU=34% | Temp=52.1C | RAM=48% | Disk=12% | Load=0.98

docs.title: 5. Final Results
docs.text:
After the test stops, Behave prints organized reports:

  • Successful/failed clients
  • Download duration
  • Ping success
  • Router response consistency

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text:
• Raspberry Pi 4/3 with 2GB+ RAM recommended
  • SD Card 16GB+

docs.title: Software
docs.text:
• Python 3.9+
  • Linux with network namespace support
  • Behave
  • MkDocs / MkDocs Material

docs.title: Network Requirements
docs.text:
• Router connected via Ethernet (preferred)
  • Internet access for ping/google tests
  • Download URL accessible

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
This project provides:

  • Fully automated router stress testing
  • Real-time health monitoring
  • Raspberry-Pi-driven load simulation
  • Professional documentation with MkDocs

You now have a full overview of the entire system. Next pages will go deeper into setup, usage, and internals.

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)
docs.text: This FAQ covers all common questions developers face while using the Stress-Test-Router automation suite. If you are stuck, start here.

docs.title: 1. What does this project actually do?
docs.text:
This system creates multiple virtual clients using Linux network namespaces + macvlan and performs:

  • Parallel ping tests

  • Parallel download tests

  • Router stress testing

  • Real-time Raspberry Pi health monitoring

It simulates real world network load on a router.

docs.title: 2. Does this work on Windows?
docs.text:
No. Windows does not support macvlan or Linux namespaces.

✔ Works on:

  • Raspberry Pi (Recommended)

  • Ubuntu / Debian Linux

  • Any native Linux machine

  • VirtualBox Ubuntu VM (with bridged adapter)

Does not work on:

  • Windows

  • WSL

  • Docker Desktop (Macvlan restricted)

docs.title: 3. Why do some clients not get an IP address?
docs.text:
Possible reasons:

  • Router DHCP service is overloaded

  • Router DHCP pool is exhausted

  • Wrong interface configured

  • Slow DHCP response

  • Network interference

  • macvlan mode restrictions

See Troubleshooting → “Clients Not Receiving IP”.

docs.title: 4. How many clients can I realistically create?
docs.text:
It depends on:

Device Recommended Max Namespaces Raspberry Pi Zero 2W 10–15 Raspberry Pi 3B+ 25–40 Raspberry Pi 4 (2GB/4GB) 40–75 Ubuntu PC 100+

More clients → more CPU usage because each namespace runs dhclient, ping, wget, etc.

docs.title: 5. Is it safe to run on my router?
docs.text:
Yes, the test is designed to be non-destructive. But note:

  • Parallel downloads may consume bandwidth

  • Router CPU may temporarily spike

  • Weak routers may reboot under heavy load

It does NOT:

  • Change router configuration

  • Attack the router

  • Modify firmware

docs.title: 6. Why do downloads fail randomly?
docs.text:
Most common reasons:

  • Router is overloaded

  • DNS not set inside namespace

  • URL server throttles requests

  • Timeout too small

Fix DNS:

echo -e "nameserver 8.8.8.8\nnameserver 1.1.1.1" | sudo tee /etc/netns/nsX/resolv.conf

docs.title: 7. Why does mkdocs serve not reload live?
docs.text:
On Windows, MkDocs has issues with:

  • OneDrive synced folders

  • VS Code auto-save delay

  • File locking problems

Fix: Move project out of OneDrive.

docs.title: 8. Why does behave skip scenarios?
docs.text:
If namespace creation fails, we intentionally skip the scenario to avoid corrupt state.

Check logs:

Skipping scenario due to failed client creation

Fix in Network Troubleshooting.

docs.title: 9. Where are the logs stored?
docs.text:
Logs are printed to console and also saved in:

/logs/stress_test.log

(if you enabled file logging in your logger settings)

docs.title: 10. Why does Pi health monitoring freeze?
docs.text:
Monitor slows down when:

  • Too many namespaces are running

  • CPU hits 80%+

  • Temperature >80°C

  • vcgencmd is missing

Install:

sudo apt install -y libraspberrypi-bin

docs.title: 11. Why am I getting “permission denied”?
docs.text:
Use sudo:

sudo behave sudo python3 main.py

Linux namespaces require elevated privileges.

docs.title: 12. Can I test wireless clients?
docs.text:
Not with macvlan. macvlan works only on Ethernet interface.

For Wi-Fi simulation:

  • Use mac80211_hwsim

  • Or multiple USB Wi-Fi dongles

docs.title: 13. Can I export documentation as PDF?
FAQ
docs.text:
MkDocs Material supports it. Run:

pip install mkdocs-with-pdf

Then run:

mkdocs build

A site/pdf/ directory will be created.

docs.title: 14. Can I add more test modules?
docs.text:
Absolutely.

This project is modular. Create new Python classes inside utils/ or managers/.

Example:

managers/ speedtest_manager.py authentication_manager.py packetloss_manager.py

docs.title: 15. Why does WSL not support macvlan?
docs.text:
Because WSL2 runs on a virtual NIC and does not allow:

  • Promiscuous mode

  • Bridging

  • Macvlan creation

Use native Linux or Pi.

docs.title: 16. Does the project need root access?
docs.text: Yes — namespace, macvlan, and dhclient all require root.

docs.title: 17. Can this damage the router?
docs.text:
No, but:

  • Very weak routers may reboot under load

  • It may saturate bandwidth temporarily

This is normal for stress testing.

docs.title: 18. Can this be used for enterprise routers?
docs.text:
Yes. It works with:

  • MikroTik

  • TP-Link

  • Netgear

  • D-Link

  • ASUS

  • Cisco home-grade routers

  • Ubiquiti (with DHCP enabled)

This is a DHCP + ICMP + download stress tester — works with any DHCP-enabled router.

docs.title: Project layout
docs.text:
Stress-test-router
    .github/
        workflows/
            ci.yml
    docs/
        docs/
            index.md  # The documentation homepage.
        mkdocs.yml    # The configuration file.
    features/

            background_steps.py
            client_creation_steps.py
            connectivity_steps.py
            download_steps.py
            google_ping_steps.py
            verify_ip_steps.py

        google_ping.feature
        parallel_download.feature
        environment.py
    src/
        client_manager.py
        download_manager.py
        ping_manager.py
    utils/
        command_runner.py
        logger.py
        pi_health_check.py
    .flake8
    .gitignore
    config.yaml
    README.md
    requirements.txt

docs.title: Troubleshooting Guide
docs.text: This page provides solutions for common problems encountered while running the Stress-Test-Router automation suite on Raspberry Pi or Linux systems. If something breaks, this is the first place to check.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Namespace creation succeeds, but no IP is assigned.

  • Logs show:

    did not get IP within X seconds

  • Scenario is aborted due to failed namespace creation.

docs.title: 1. DHCP Server on Router Not Responding
docs.text:
Make sure your router is: - Powered on - Not overloaded - Has DHCP enabled

Fix: Reboot the router and try again:

sudo systemctl restart dhcpcd

docs.title: 2. Physical Interface Name Incorrect
docs.text:
Check your Pi’s interface name:

ip link

Typical values: - eth0 - wlan0

Make sure your config.yaml uses the correct interface:

interface: eth0

docs.title: 3. DHCP Client Not Installed
docs.text:
Install it:

sudo apt install -y isc-dhcp-client

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text: • Download manager reports: [FAIL] nsX download failed

docs.title: 1. Router Has No Internet
docs.text:
Test from host:

ping 8.8.8.8

docs.title: 2. DNS Failing
docs.text:
Fix by rewriting resolver file for all namespaces:

echo -e "nameserver 8.8.8.8\nnameserver 1.1.1.1" | sudo tee /etc/resolv.conf

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Clients cannot ping router or 8.8.8.8

  • Behavior inconsistent

docs.title: 1. Enable IP Forwarding
docs.text: sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1

docs.title: 2. Flush iptables
docs.text: sudo iptables -F sudo iptables -t nat -F

docs.title: 3. Check namespace interface state
docs.text:
sudo ip netns exec ns1 ip link show

Interface must be UP.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Markdown changes not reflected
  • mkdocs serve only updates on restart

docs.title: 1. Ensure you’re running from the root mkdocs directory
docs.text:
Correct: Stress-test-router/docs/

Run: mkdocs serve

docs.title: 2. Disable OneDrive Sync Conflicts (Windows)
docs.text:
Move your project out of OneDrive:

C:\Projects\Stress-test-router

MkDocs often fails to watch files inside OneDrive.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Steps not running
  • Behave says: Step not found

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
Check folder structure:

features/steps/*.py

Make sure your step function names match your .feature files.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• vcgencmd not found
  • Health monitor throws warnings

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
Install Pi utilities:

sudo apt install -y libraspberrypi-bin

To test:

vcgencmd measure_temp

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Namespace creation fails
  • dhclient fails
  • ping fails inside namespace

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
Run with sudo:

sudo behave

or:

sudo python3 main.py

docs.title: 8. WSL Users: Macvlan Will Not Work
Symptoms
docs.text:
• ip link add macvlan... fails
  • Pings fail

docs.title: Reason
docs.text: WSL does not support macvlan.

docs.title: Fix:
docs.text:
Run on: - Raspberry Pi - Linux Machine

  • VirtualBox + Ubuntu with bridged adapter

WSL is not supported.

docs.title: 9. Router Blocks ICMP or Wget Traffic
Symptoms
docs.text:
• Pings work from host but not from namespace
  • Download test fails

docs.title: Fixes:
docs.text:
• Disable firewall in router
  • Disable parental control
  • Disable guest network isolation

docs.title: Fix
docs.text:
Run:

sudo ip -all netns delete sudo rm -rf /etc/netns/*

docs.title: Installation Guide
docs.text: This Section explains how to install, configure, and prepare the Stress-Test-Router project on any Linux system or Raspberry Pi device.

docs.title: 1. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/panditpankaj21/Stress-test-router.git
cd Stress-test-router

docs.title: 2. Install Python Dependencies
docs.text:
Make sure Python 3.9+ is installed.

pip install -r requirements.txt

If you are on Raspberry Pi and need system-level privileges:

sudo pip3 install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: 3. Install System Packages (Linux / Raspberry Pi)
docs.text:
The project uses tools like ip, iproute2, macvlan, and networking utilities.

sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y python3 python3-pip iproute2 net-tools git wget

Additional recommended packages:

sudo apt install -y dnsutils htop nmap

docs.title: Run ALL feature tests:
docs.text: sudo behave

docs.title: Run a specific feature:
docs.text: sudo behave features/<feature_name>.feature

docs.title: Run by tag:
docs.text: sudo behave --tags="@tag_name"

docs.title: Documentation Setup (MkDocs)
docs.text: The project includes a full documentation system using MkDocs + Material Theme.

docs.title: Enter docs directory:
docs.text: cd docs

docs.title: Start live preview:
docs.text:
mkdocs serve

Documentation is available at:

http://127.0.0.1:8000

docs.title: Build documentation:
docs.text: mkdocs build

docs.title: Show help:
docs.text: mkdocs -h

docs.title: 🎉 Installation Complete!
docs.text: You are now ready to run network stress tests and explore full documentation.

docs.title: Raspberry Pi Setup Guide
docs.text: This guide explains how to correctly prepare a Raspberry Pi for running the Stress‑Test‑Router project. Follow each step carefully to ensure stable network simulation, macvlan creation, and health monitoring during stress tests.

docs.title: Supported Raspberry Pi Models
docs.text:
The project has been tested and works perfectly on:

  • Raspberry Pi 3B+
  • Raspberry Pi 4 (4GB/8GB)
  • Raspberry Pi Zero 2W
  • Raspberry Pi CM4

Any Pi running Raspberry Pi OS (32‑bit or 64‑bit) is supported.

docs.title: 1. Update and Upgrade the System
docs.text:
Before installing anything, update your Pi:

sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y

docs.title: 2. Install Required System Packages
docs.text:
These tools are required for networking, namespaces, and monitoring.

sudo apt install -y python3 python3-pip iproute2 net-tools git wget                     dnsutils htop nmap

docs.title: 3. Install Python Dependencies
docs.text:
Install required Python packages globally or with --user.

sudo pip3 install behave mkdocs mkdocs-material psutil

(If you cloned the project, dependencies will also come from requirements.txt.)

docs.title: 4. Test Basic Network Commands
docs.text:
Ensure Pi supports ping and DNS resolution:

ping -c 3 google.com
dig google.com

docs.title: 5. Recommended Monitoring Tools
docs.text:
Although the project prints system health during tests, these tools help during debugging:

sudo apt install -y sysstat btop iotop vnstat

Run:

top
btop
vnstat

🎉You are all set now got to Installation Process.

docs.title: Download Manager
docs.text: Handles high-load parallel downloads inside isolated Linux network namespaces while continuously monitoring Raspberry Pi system health.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Download Manager simulates large-scale download operations from multiple virtual network clients created using Linux network namespaces (ip netns).

docs.title: It performs:
docs.text:
• Parallel HTTP/HTTPS downloads
  • Per-namespace isolation
  • Time-bound transfers using timeout
  • Performance measurement (duration, interruptions)
  • Continuous 2-second interval Raspberry Pi health monitoring (CPU, RAM, Temp, Load)
  • Consolidated result reporting

docs.title: Typical Use Cases:
docs.text:
• Stress-testing a router’s bandwidth distribution
  • Measuring network reliability under load
  • Running heavy download tests with 10–50+ virtual clients
  • Observing thermal and resource impact on Raspberry Pi during network pressure

docs.title: Parallel Execution
docs.text: Each namespace downloads the same file simultaneously.

docs.title: Timeout Protection
docs.text: If a download takes longer than the configured limit, the OS kills the attempt.

docs.title: Resource Monitoring
docs.text: A background task prints real-time Pi health: CPU | Temp | RAM | Disk | Load

docs.title: Accurate Performance Metrics
docs.text:
For each namespace:

Metric Description success Did download finish before timeout? duration Total time effort (seconds) interrupted True if download was force-killed stderr/stdout Diagnostic information

docs.title: 1. worker()
docs.text: Handles a single client's download: - Executes wget inside the network namespace - Measures time taken - Stores result in dictionary - Logs success/failure

docs.title: 2. start_parallel_download()
docs.text:
• Starts a health monitor task
  • Spawns download tasks for each namespace
  • Waits for all downloads
  • Stops health monitor
  • Returns structured output

docs.title: 3. health_worker()
docs.text: Runs asynchronously in the background to report Raspberry Pi performance every 2 seconds.

docs.title: Example Behave Step Usage
docs.text:
@when("all clients start parallel download")
def step_download(context):
    url = context.config.get("test_download_url")
    timeout = context.config.get("download_timeout")

    dm = DownloadManager(url, timeout)

    context.results = asyncio.run(
        dm.start_parallel_download(context.net_mgr.client_namespaces)
    )

docs.title: Full Code — Download Manager
docs.text:
python
import asyncio
import time
from utils.logger import logger
from utils.pi_health_check import health_worker

async def run_cmd(cmd, suppress_output=False):
    proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_shell(
        cmd,
        stdout=(
            asyncio.subprocess.DEVNULL if suppress_output else asyncio.subprocess.PIPE
        ),
        stderr=(
            asyncio.subprocess.DEVNULL if suppress_output else asyncio.subprocess.PIPE
        ),
    )
    stdout, stderr = await proc.communicate()

    return {
        "returncode": proc.returncode,
        "stdout": None if suppress_output else stdout.decode(),
        "stderr": None if suppress_output else stderr.decode(),
    }

class DownloadManager:
    def __init__(self, url, timeout):
        self.url = url
        self.timeout = timeout

    async def worker(self, ns, results):
        cmd = (
            f"sudo ip netns exec {ns} "
            f"timeout {self.timeout} wget -q "
            f"-O /dev/null -o /dev/null "
            f"--no-cache {self.url}"
        )

        start = time.time()
        result = await run_cmd(cmd)

        duration = time.time() - start
        success = result["returncode"] == 0

        results[ns] = {
            "success": success,
            "duration": duration,
            "interrupted": not success,
            "stdout": result["stdout"],
            "stderr": result["stderr"],
        }

        if success:
            logger.info(f"[OK] {ns} completed download in {duration:.2f} sec")
        else:
            logger.error(f"[FAIL] {ns} download failed after {duration:.2f} sec")

    async def start_parallel_download(self, namespaces):
        results = {}

        stop_event = asyncio.Event()
        tasks = [self.worker(ns, results) for ns in namespaces]

        health_task = asyncio.create_task(health_worker(stop_event))

        await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

        stop_event.set()
        await health_task

        return results

docs.title: Outputs Returned by DownloadManager
docs.text:
Example result structure:

json
{
  "ns1": {
    "success": true,
    "duration": 2.314,
    "interrupted": false,
    "stdout": "",
    "stderr": ""
  },
  "ns2": {
    "success": false,
    "duration": 5.000,
    "interrupted": true,
    "stdout": "",
    "stderr": "timeout expired"
  }
}

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Use a fast mirror (e.g., Ubuntu ISO, sample files) for testing.

  • Avoid extremely small files (results become unrealistic).

  • Run high-load tests on Raspberry Pi 4 for accurate performance.

  • Keep passive cooling or heatsinks attached.

  • Avoid using Wi-Fi if testing router Ethernet performance.

docs.title: Network Namespace Management
docs.text:
This document explains how network namespaces are created, configured, and used in this project to simulate multiple isolated clients on a Raspberry Pi.

It includes:

  • Concept overview
  • CLI commands for manual namespace creation
  • Full implementation code for the NetworkManager class
  • Usage patterns inside Behave feature tests

docs.title: What Are Network Namespaces?
docs.text:
Linux network namespaces allow isolation of networking stacks. Each namespace has its own:

  • Interfaces
  • Routing tables
  • IP addresses
  • DNS resolver config

In this project, namespaces simulate multiple independent clients, each with its own MAC, IP, and routing — perfect for router stress testing.

docs.title: How We Create Clients
docs.text:
We use the following strategy:

  • Create a namespace: ns1, ns2, ...
  • Create a macvlan interface inside each namespace
  • Assign unique MAC addresses
  • Request IP from router using dhclient
  • Add DNS resolvers

docs.title: Manual Commands (For Understanding)
docs.text: Below are the raw Linux commands the code automates.

docs.title: Create a namespace:
docs.text: sudo ip netns add ns1

docs.title: Create a macvlan linked to the parent interface:
docs.text: sudo ip link add link eth0 macvlan1 type macvlan mode bridge

docs.title: Move the macvlan into namespace:
docs.text: sudo ip link set macvlan1 netns ns1

docs.title: Bring interfaces up:
docs.text:
sudo ip netns exec ns1 ip link set dev macvlan1 up
sudo ip netns exec ns1 ip link set lo up

docs.title: Request IP from router:
docs.text:
sudo ip netns exec ns1 dhclient -v macvlan1 \
    -pf /run/dhclient-ns1.pid \
    -lf /var/lib/dhcp/dhclient-ns1.leases

docs.title: Ping from the namespace:
docs.text: sudo ip netns exec ns1 ping -c 1 8.8.8.8

docs.title: Delete namespace:
docs.text: sudo ip netns delete ns1

docs.title: Full NetworkManager Code
docs.text:
Below is the full implementation used in this project.

<CODE_PLACEHOLDER>

docs.title: Namespace Cleanup Logic
docs.text:
If any namespace fails to get an IP, the system:

  • Releases DHCP leases
  • Deletes macvlan interfaces
  • Deletes namespace folders
  • Resets all state

Ensures clean environment for every test run.

docs.title: How It’s Used Inside Behave
docs.text:
Example:

Given I create 10 virtual clients using macvlan
When all clients attempt to ping Google DNS
Then all clients should respond successfully

The step file calls:

context.net_mgr.create_clients(10)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Each namespace gets a unique MAC address.
  • DNS is injected using /etc/netns/<ns>/resolv.conf.
  • All commands run in async for parallel creation.

Add your full code below where <CODE_PLACEHOLDER> is.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Ping Manager performs high-concurrency ICMP tests from multiple network namespaces. Each namespace sends repeated ping requests to a target IP for a fixed duration. The module also runs a background health monitor to detect system stalls or Pi overloads.

Designed for: Router stress testing, DHCP validation, multiclient connectivity, and real-time network health analysis.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Parallel ping execution across unlimited namespaces
  • Configurable duration and target IP
  • Micro-jitter between pings to avoid burst synchronization
  • Integrated Raspberry Pi/host health monitoring using health_worker
  • Clean async architecture using asyncio
  • Behave BDD step definitions included

docs.title: Full Code
docs.text:
import asyncio
import random
import time
from utils.pi_health_check import health_worker
from utils.logger import logger


async def run_cmd(cmd, suppress_output=False):
    proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_shell(
        cmd,
        stdout=(
            asyncio.subprocess.DEVNULL if suppress_output else asyncio.subprocess.PIPE
        ),
        stderr=(
            asyncio.subprocess.DEVNULL if suppress_output else asyncio.subprocess.PIPE
        ),
    )
    stdout, stderr = await proc.communicate()

    return {
        "returncode": proc.returncode,
        "stdout": None if suppress_output else stdout.decode(),
        "stderr": None if suppress_output else stderr.decode(),
    }


class PingManager:
    def __init__(self, target_ip, ping_duration):
        self.target_ip = target_ip
        self.duration = ping_duration

    async def worker(self, ns, end_time, results):
        success = True

        while time.time() < end_time:
            result = await run_cmd(
                f"sudo ip netns exec {ns} ping -c 1 -W 1 {self.target_ip}"
            )

            if result["returncode"] != 0:
                success = False
                logger.error(f"[FAIL] {ns} cannot reach {self.target_ip}")

            # jitter: prevents all namespaces pinging at exact same moment
            await asyncio.sleep(random.random() * 0.05)

        results[ns] = success

    async def run_test(self, namespaces):
        end_time = time.time() + self.duration
        results = {}

        stop_event = asyncio.Event()

        ping_tasks = [self.worker(ns, end_time, results) for ns in namespaces]

        # background health monitoring
        health_task = asyncio.create_task(health_worker(stop_event))

        await asyncio.gather(*ping_tasks)

        stop_event.set()
        await health_task

        return results

docs.title: Behave Step Definitions
docs.text:
@when("all clients attempt to ping the router simultaneously")
def step_ping_clients(context):
    logger.info("------ PARALLEL CLIENT PING STARTED -----")

    ping_duration = context.config.get("ping_duration")

    pm = PingManager(context.router_ip, ping_duration)

    context.results = asyncio.run(
        pm.run_test([ns for ns in context.net_mgr.client_namespaces])
    )

    logger.info("----- STOPPED PING -----")


@when("all clients attempt to ping Google DNS")
def step_ping_google(context):
    logger.info("------ PARALLEL CLIENT PING TO GOOGLE DNS STARTED -----")

    google_dns_ip = "8.8.8.8"
    ping_duration = context.config.get("ping_duration")

    pm = PingManager(google_dns_ip, ping_duration)

    context.results = asyncio.run(
        pm.run_test([ns for ns in context.net_mgr.client_namespaces])
    )

    logger.info("----- STOPPED PING TO GOOGLE DNS -----")

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Set ping_duration in your YAML config.
  • All namespaces are fetched dynamically from your NetworkManager (context.net_mgr).
  • Use this in combination with download_manager, throughput_manager, or multi-stage stress suites.

If you want an A+ version for tcp_manager.md, throughput_manager.md, or a master README, just ask!
